### Post by yopnono on 2006-09-20
First make sure you uninstall/remove the 915resolution you installed by yourself, then...

install the 915resolution from synaptic. Then edit the "/etc/default/915resolution" file to setup your resolution.

---

### Post by FreeZey on 2006-09-20
the one i got was from synaptic... thats how the tutorial tells you to get it

---

### Post by yopnono on 2006-09-20
just install and edit the "/etc/default/915resolution" file. Then reboot

Don't set any startup script the wiki is *outdated*

The 915 deb have all boot scripts needed.

---

### Post by FreeZey on 2006-09-20
ok so i sudo apt-get remove 915resolution... then i sudo apt-get install 915resolution... done... then i opened the conf file used 5a and then rebooted and still nothingg...didnt change nething its still runing at 1024x768

---

### Post by yopnono on 2006-09-20
Ok well I have installed the 915 many times and never had any problems.
Try this remove it using synaptic and *complete remove* 915.

Then open terminal and 
```
sudo updatedb
```

Then use the search to find any file called 915resolution something in "file system" and delete them. You see if you did create your own boot script then they may interfere.

So just make sure all 915 files are gone.

then install it again. and edit the conf file. mine looks like this.
```

#
# 915resolution default
#
# find free modes by  /usr/sbin/915resolution -l
# and set it to MODE
# e.g. use MODE=54 
MODE=3a
#
# and set resolutions for the mode.
# e.g. use XRESO=1024 and YRESO=768
XRESO=1400
YRESO=1050
#
# We can also set the pixel mode.
# e.g. use BIT=32
# Please note that this is optional,
# you can also leave this value blank.
BIT=
```

After reboot and login, if it don't work press CTRL+ALT+F8 to see if the 915 started. Switch back using CTRL+ALT+F7

---

### Post by yopnono on 2006-09-20
hum. try to put this in the /etc/init.d/bootmisc.sh

```
/usr/sbin/915resolution 3a 1400 1050
```

Just after:
```
[ -z "$DELAYLOGIN" ] && DELAYLOGIN=yes
[ -z "$EDITMOTD" ] && EDITMOTD=yes
[ -f /etc/default/rcS ] && . /etc/default/rcS
```

---

### Post by yopnono on 2006-09-20
Also remove all other resolutions that you don't need in the xorg file
Should not be needed, but...

---

### Post by FreeZey on 2006-09-21
Well I got it figured out a frien came by and took a look at it... he turned on lcd display on the i810switch and then tryed a different resolution it was ahigher one and it worked
